one_line_takeaway: LLM-based vulnerability detectors embedded in CI/CD gates can have their high clean accuracy almost entirely nullified (Complete Resistance as low as 0.12%) by innocuous, compiler-preserving edits like renaming a variable or adding a disabled #ifdef block, and universal adversarial strings optimized cheaply on a 14B open-weight surrogate transfer effectively to closed-source APIs including GPT-4o.
key_finding: Clean benchmark recall does not predict deployment robustness: across five of six models, Complete Resistance (CR) is at most 13%, meaning over 87% of correctly-detected vulnerabilities can be evaded by at least one syntax-preserving edit. Concrete numbers from Table 1: CodeAstra achieves 69.08% clean TPR but on-target attacks reduce CR to 0.12% (only 4 of 3,454 correctly-flagged vulnerabilities survive all carriers, a 99.88% union ASR); Llama3.1-8B on-target CR is 0.13% (union ASR 99.87%); StarCoder2-15B has the highest robustness among white-box models at CR=9.61% under on-target optimization. Under surrogate transfer, black-box/gray-box models show union ASRcond of 44.04% (GPT-5-mini) to 99.38% (Qwen2.5-Coder-32B), with GPT-4o at 87.00% (CR=13.00%); GPT-5-mini is a notable outlier with substantially lower ASR (44.04%) and per-carrier ASRs of only 10.6-28.1%, and this lower transferability held up even when identifier-substitution strings were re-optimized on the stronger Qwen2.5-Coder-32B surrogate (ASR rose only 10.62%->13.4%). Per-carrier breakdown (Table 2, transfer setting) shows preprocessor carriers (#ifdef, macro) dominate for most models (e.g. #ifdef 99.03% on Llama3.1-8B, 98.84% on Qwen2.5-Coder-32B) while StarCoder2-15B shows a single-carrier dependency (macro alone: 86.83% vs. <5% on all other carriers). GCG-optimized attacks strongly outperform non-optimized baselines: random token insertion via comment carrier achieves 13.2% ASRcond vs. 91.5% for GCG under identical conditions; random identifier substitution achieves only 7-30% vs. much higher on-target/optimized rates. On defenses: input sanitization (stripping comments/preprocessor directives) drives comment/ifdef/macro ASR to zero but leaves identifier-substitution and dead-branch carriers intact, and induces clean-accuracy drift (e.g. Llama3.1-8B clean TP rises from 1552 to 1685 after stripping, an 8.6% increase, indicating reliance on non-executable tokens as decision features). Randomized smoothing (SmoothLLM-style, K=5 identifier-renaming variants + majority vote) yields only modest ASR reductions (Llama3.1-8B: 43.49%->34.57%, -8.9pp; CodeAstra: 85.41%->82.71%, -2.7pp; StarCoder2-15B: 16.27%->14.63%, -1.6pp). Adversarial LoRA fine-tuning on Llama3.1-8B raises CR from 0.13% to 3.88% and sharply cuts comment/preprocessor ASR (comment: 42.01%->0.16%; ifdef: 99.03%->0.00%) but identifier-substitution ASR increases (43.49%->89.56%) and clean TPR drops 6.3pp (31.04%->24.72%), demonstrating a carrier-shifting robustness-accuracy trade-off with no single sufficient defense. All models achieve 100% benign-class invariance (attacks do not corrupt benign predictions into false positives), confirming the flips are targeted evasions rather than indiscriminate label noise.
core_contribution: The paper (titled 'Syntax- and Compilation-Preserving Evasion of LLM Vulnerability Detectors', by Sun, Oprea, Wong) introduces a systematic evaluation of detection-time integrity for LLM-based C/C++ vulnerability detectors under a syntax-preserving evasion threat model. It contributes: (1) carrier-constrained GCG optimization -- gradient-guided universal adversarial string search restricted to four syntax/compilation-preserving carrier families (identifier substitution, comments, preprocessor macros/#ifdef, dead-branch code) via automated Tree-sitter and gcc validation; (2) a new metric, Complete Resistance (CR), the fraction of correctly-detected vulnerabilities that survive ALL five evaluated attack variants simultaneously (as opposed to per-attack Attack Success Rate); (3) a unified benchmark, UNIFIED-VUL-N (N=5000), pooled from PrimeVul (3000), BigVul (1000), and DiverseVul (1000) with cross-source SHA-256 deduplication; and (4) an empirical demonstration that universal adversarial strings optimized on a 14B open-weight surrogate (Qwen2.5-Coder-14B) transfer to black-box APIs (GPT-4o, GPT-5-mini) without further optimization. The authors also evaluate three defenses (input sanitization, randomized smoothing adapted from SmoothLLM, and adversarial LoRA fine-tuning) and show none is sufficient alone.
threat_model: CI/CD evasion scenario: an adversary controls existing vulnerable C/C++ code already flagged VULNERABLE by an automated LLM-based scanner embedded as a pass/fail gate in a CI/CD pipeline (no human review). The adversary does not introduce a new vulnerability; they apply source-level, non-interfering (non-executable or unreachable) edits -- identifier renaming, comment insertion, preprocessor macro/#ifdef insertion, dead-branch insertion -- that must be syntax-preserving (Tree-sitter-validated) and largely compilation-preserving (gcc-validated), while leaving the underlying vulnerability intact, to flip the detector's output to BENIGN. Three access regimes are evaluated: white-box (full gradient access, on-target GCG), black-box API via surrogate transfer (strings optimized on Qwen2.5-Coder-14B applied unmodified to GPT-4o/GPT-5-mini), and cost-limited gray-box (weights accessible but on-target optimization computationally prohibitive, e.g. ~20 GPU-hours per carrier on a 32B model on a single H100). Scope explicitly excludes human code review and ensemble systems combining LLM reasoning with classical static/dynamic analysis; formal runtime-equivalence is not claimed, only syntax/compilation preservation.
limitations: Explicitly stated by the authors: (1) scope restricted to C/C++ with a fixed prompting protocol -- generalization to other languages untested; (2) the three-tier validation certifies syntax- and compilation-level preservation only, not formal runtime equivalence, because function-level datasets lack full execution environments; (3) optimized universal strings (especially for identifier substitution) are often unnatural-looking and could plausibly be caught by style linters or repository naming policies -- the reported attack success rates assume no such pre-screening; (4) multi-seed statistical analysis covers only on-target identifier substitution across three models, so variance for other carriers/regimes is unquantified; (5) identifier-substitution compilation preservation is only 90.9% (131/1,434 failures from symbol collisions with injected headers), lower than the 100% achieved by the other four carriers; (6) Tier-3 gcc compilation validation covers CodeAstra predictions only (generalized to other models via the argument that transfer strings are universal, not independently verified per-model); (7) several detectors (CodeAstra: 70% FPR, GPT-5-mini: 54% FPR on benign inputs) are flagged by the authors themselves as questionable standalone deployment gates even before adversarial pressure is applied, which the paper notes as context for interpreting the robustness results.

datasets_used: UNIFIED-VUL-N (N=5000): authors' own pooled/deduplicated benchmark combining PrimeVul (3000 functions), BigVul (1000 functions), DiverseVul (1000 functions), C/C++ only, deduplicated via cross-source SHA-256 hashing on normalized code, token-length filtered to <=4096 tokens (Qwen2.5-Coder-14B tokenizer)

Compilable Validation Set: 2,000 ground-truth vulnerable functions sampled from PrimeVul's compilable subset, verified to compile standalone via gcc -fsyntax-only; 1,434 of these correctly detected by CodeAstra used for Tier-3 compilation validation
Held-out benign set: 500 ground-truth benign functions used for false-positive-rate and benign-class-invariance evaluation
quantitative_results: CodeAstra: clean TPR 69.08%, on-target attack CR 0.12% (4/3454 vulnerabilities survive all carriers), union ASRcond 99.88%
Llama3.1-8B: clean TPR 31.04%, on-target CR 0.13%, union ASRcond 99.87%
StarCoder2-15B: clean TPR 99.04%, on-target CR 9.61% (highest among white-box models), union ASRcond 90.39%
GPT-4o: clean TPR (transfer setting) 46.00%, CR 13.00%, union ASRcond 87.00%
GPT-5-mini: clean TPR 73.62%, CR 55.96% (highest robustness of all six models), union ASRcond 44.04%

Qwen2.5-Coder-32B: clean TPR 22.42% (transfer), union ASRcond 99.38%, CR 0.62%; on-target identifier-substitution ASR 92.60% vs 26.94% via 14B surrogate transfer (capacity-dependent transferability: 0.5B/1.5B surrogates give only 6-7%)
GCG (comment carrier) vs random token insertion: 91.5% vs 13.2% ASRcond
Random identifier substitution baseline: 7-30% ASRcond across models (vs. much higher for optimized attacks)
Output-format-injection baseline (prompt injection, outside threat model): 55-99% success
Input sanitization defense: drives comment/ifdef/macro ASR to 0%, but Llama3.1-8B clean detections rise from 1552 to 1685 TP (+8.6%) due to prediction drift
Randomized smoothing (SmoothLLM-style, K=5): ASR reduction of only -8.9pp (Llama3.1-8B, 43.49%->34.57%), -2.7pp (CodeAstra, 85.41%->82.71%), -1.6pp (StarCoder2-15B, 16.27%->14.63%)

Adversarial LoRA fine-tuning (Llama3.1-8B): CR improves 0.13%->3.88%; comment ASR 42.01%->0.16%; ifdef ASR 99.03%->0.00%; macro ASR 65.46%->16.91%; but identifier-substitution ASR increases 43.49%->89.56% and dead-branch remains high (86.66%->68.20%); clean TPR drops 31.04%->24.72% (-6.3pp)
Benign-class invariance: 100% across all models and attacks (no false-positive corruption)
Benign-side clean FPR: CodeAstra 70.0%, GPT-5-mini 54.4%, GPT-4o 36.2%, Llama3.1-8B 16.8%, Qwen2.5-Coder-32B 11.0% (out of 500 benign functions)
Multi-seed ASRcond (on-target identifier substitution, 3 seeds): CodeAstra 80.7%+/-9.1, Llama3.1-8B 31.1%+/-11.3, StarCoder2-15B 17.6%+/-6.8
baselines_compared: Random identifier substitution (unoptimized, K=3 seeds, mean+/-std reported)
Random token insertion via comment carrier (vocabulary-sampled tokens, no gradient optimization)

Output-format-injection / prompt-injection baseline (JSON-spoofing in comments; explicitly framed as outside the paper's syntax-preserving threat model, testing a distinct output-harness vulnerability)
Randomized smoothing defense adapted from SmoothLLM (Robey et al. 2024)
Adversarial LoRA fine-tuning defense (carrier-diverse training, related to Madry et al. 2019 adversarial training)
